planting.note: Root stem cuttings in barely moist, well-drained cactus mix or train established plants on a trellis or through a tree in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; queen of the night requires excellent drainage — root rot in wet soil is the primary cause of plant failure.

directions: Root stem cuttings in well-drained cactus mix or plant on a trellis in partial shade to full sun; queen of the night is a Jamaican and Central American epiphytic cactus that is grown for one of the most spectacular and fleeting floral events in horticulture — its enormous flowers, reaching 12 inches or more in diameter, open only once per year, only at night (typically between midnight and 3 a.m.), filling the surrounding air with a powerful, exotic, vanilla-like fragrance, then closing and withering before dawn. The vine itself is a sprawling, multi-stemmed, vinelike cactus that climbs trees, fences, and walls by aerial roots; its flat, jointed stems are unattractive in comparison to the drama of its annual flower event. Dedicated enthusiasts stay up all night to witness the blooming, and the anticipation and communal watching of the opening flower is a celebrated garden ritual in subtropical and tropical neighborhoods. Plants may take several years from cutting to first bloom. In zones 9–12 it grows outdoors on walls and trees; elsewhere it is a large container or greenhouse specimen. A plant whose horticultural impact is entirely disproportionate to its one annual bloom event.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings (wear gloves — milky latex is an irritant) or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade after frost; Caribbean copper plant grows fastest and colors most intensely in full sun; partial shade produces somewhat less vivid coloring but is acceptable.

directions: Root stem cuttings (caution — wear gloves, latex is a skin and eye irritant) or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; Caribbean copper plant is a tropical American shrub or small tree grown entirely for its spectacular, deep-mahogany to wine-red, rounded foliage — the constantly produced, oval, alternate leaves emerge in brilliant coppery-red and mature to deep burgundy-maroon, maintaining their rich coloring throughout the season. In the tropical garden it provides a bold, jewel-toned color anchor that contrasts dramatically with lime-green foliage (aralia, euphorbia tirucalli 'Sticks on Fire'), silver-gray plants, and orange-flowered tropical perennials. It is fast-growing to 10–15 feet in tropical climates, functioning as a screening shrub or specimen small tree; in cooler zones 9 gardens it may be damaged by hard frost but resprouts from the roots. Insignificant yellowish flowers. All parts contain toxic, milky latex — handle with gloves and avoid eye contact. Excellent as a bold color contrast in mixed tropical borders.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade after frost; Okinawan spinach produces new growth continuously and can be harvested by snipping stem tips every few weeks — the new growth is most tender and flavorful.

directions: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix and plant in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in partial shade; Okinawan spinach (Cholesterol spinach, edible velvet plant, han tsai tai) is a tropical perennial leafy vegetable from Southeast Asia and Okinawa that is one of the most productive and effortless greens for subtropical gardens in zones 9–12 — the plant grows continuously year-round in frost-free climates, producing an inexhaustible supply of young, tender growing tips that are harvested by snipping and regrowing for months. The leaves are bicolored — dark green on the top and deep purple-red underneath, with a velvety, slightly hairy texture — creating an attractive ornamental plant that functions simultaneously as an edible garden specimen. Young leaves and tips are eaten raw in salads (pleasant, mild flavor) or briefly sautéed. The plant is rich in antioxidants and flavonoids associated with the traditional Okinawan diet. Tolerates partial shade better than most vegetables, making it valuable for shaded garden beds where conventional leafy greens would bolt. Distinct from the related Gynura procumbens (longevity spinach) in its ornamental bicolored foliage.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in midsummer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun in spring or fall; holly olive establishes readily and requires minimal care once established — it is slower-growing than Chinese holly but ultimately more fragrant and shade-tolerant.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun; holly olive (false holly, holly-leaved osmanthus) is a versatile Japanese evergreen shrub that combines two highly desirable landscape qualities that rarely coexist — the bold, spiny, holly-like foliage that deters deer and unwanted entry, and the extraordinarily fragrant, small, white flowers that perfume the autumn garden with a powerful, sweet, apricot-like scent similar to but distinct from the related tea olive (O. fragrans). The spiny, glossy, dark-green leaves closely resemble English holly leaves, creating a formal, polished effect in the landscape; several cultivars offer gold-, white-, or purple-tinted foliage. It tolerates partial shade, dry soil, and urban conditions far better than most broadleaf evergreens. 'Goshiki' (cream, green, and pink variegated), 'Rotundifolius' (small, rounded, spineless leaves), 'Purpureus' (deep-purple new growth), and 'Gulftide' (compact, cold-hardy) are widely grown selections. More deer-resistant than almost any comparable shrub due to the spiny foliage.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial to full shade in spring; yellow archangel spreads aggressively by long, arching, rooting stolons — site carefully away from natural areas where it can escape and become invasive. Excellent within contained bed borders.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to moderately dry, average soil in partial to full shade; yellow archangel (golden dead-nettle) is a vigorous, fast-spreading shade groundcover with one of the most attractive and persistent foliage displays in the low-maintenance shade garden — the soft, nettle-shaped leaves are heavily marked with silver variegation in patterns that retain their brilliance through winter, creating bright, light-reflecting patches of color in the darkest corners of the shaded garden throughout the year. In late spring the plant produces whorled clusters of bright yellow, hooded, snapdragon-like flowers on upright stems above the groundcover mat. It spreads with considerable vigor by long, arching stems that root wherever they touch the ground, making it an effective, weed-suppressing groundcover but potentially problematic in gardens bordering woodland areas where it has been documented as invasive in some regions of the Pacific Northwest and northeastern United States. Most commonly grown as the variegated cultivar 'Variegatum' (silver-marked) or the compact 'Hermann's Pride' (heavily silver-marked, less aggressive). Excellent for large, shaded areas under dense trees where maintaining other groundcovers would be difficult.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
